analogy: Think of "An Empire Across Three Continents" through the chapter's own opening images: 29 ii An Empire Across Three Continents Nomadic Empires empires 30 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY OVER the two millennia that followed the establishment of empires in Mesopotamia, various attempts at empire- building took place across the region and in the area to the west and... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
An Empire Across Three Continents is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 29 ii An Empire Across Three Continents Nomadic Empires empires

The text explains that 30 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY OVER the two millennia that followed the establishment of empires in Mesopotamia, various attempts at empire- building took place across the region and in the area to the west and east of it.

The text explains that by the sixth century BCE, Iranians had established control over major parts of the Assyrian empire.

The text explains that networks of trade developed overland, as well as along the coasts of the Mediterranean Sea.

The text explains that in the eastern Mediterranean, Greek cities and their colonies benefited from improvements in trade that were the result of these changes.

The text explains that they also benefited from close trade with nomadic people to the north of the Black Sea.

The text explains that in Greece, for the most part, city-states such as Athens and Sparta were the focus of civic life.

The text explains that from among the Greek states, in the late fourth century BCE, the ruler of the kingdom of Macedon, Alexander, undertook a series of military campaigns and conquered parts of North Africa, West Asia and Iran, reaching up to the Beas.

The text explains that here, his soldiers refused to proceed further east.

The text explains that alexander’s troops retreated, though many Greeks stayed behind.

The text explains that throughout the area under Alexander’s control, ideals and cultural traditions were shared amongst the Greeks and the local population.

The text explains that the region on the whole became ‘Hellenised’ (the Greeks were called Hellenes), and Greek became a well-known language throughout.

The text explains that the political unity of Alexander’s empire disintegrated quickly after his death, but for almost three centuries after, Hellenistic culture remained important in the area.

The text explains that the period is often referred to as the ‘Hellenistic period’ in the history of the region, but this ignores the way in which other cultures (especially Iranian culture associated with the old empire of Iran) were as important as – if not often more important than – Hellenistic notions and ideas.

The text explains that this section deals with important aspects of what happened after this.

The text explains that small but well-organised military forces of the central Italian city- state of Rome took advantage of the political discord that followed the disintegration of Alexander’s empire and established control over North Africa and the eastern Mediterranean from the second century BCE. empires

The text explains that all these attempts to make and maintain empires were driven by the search to control the resources of the trading networks that existed in the region as a whole, and to derive benefit from the links of the region with other areas such as India or China.

The text explains that this was partly due to disputes and conflict over resources in various regions.

The text explains that roman historians have a rich collection of sources to go on, which we can broadly divide into three groups: (a) texts, (b) documents and (c) material remains.

The text explains that textual sources include histories of the period written by contemporaries (these were usually called ‘Annals’, because the narrative was constructed on a year-by-year basis), letters, speeches, sermons, laws, and so on.

The text explains that documentary sources include mainly inscriptions and papyri.

The text explains that each of these sources can only tell us just so much about the past, and combining them can be a fruitful exercise, but how well this is done depends on the historian’s skill!

The text explains that rome dominated the Mediterranean and all the regions around that sea in both directions, north as well as south.

The text explains that although Augustus was the sole ruler and the only real source of authority, the fiction was kept alive that he was actually only the ‘leading citizen’ (Princeps in Latin), not the absolute ruler.

The text explains that the Senate hated and feared the army, because it was a source of often- unpredictable violence, especially in the tense conditions of the third century when government was forced to tax more heavily to pay for its mounting military expenditures.

The text explains that the Emperor Trajan’s Dream – A Conquest of India? ‘Then, after a winter (115/16) in Antioch marked by a great earthquake, in 116 Trajan marched down the Euphrates to Ctesiphon, the Parthian capital, and then to the head of the Persian Gulf.

The text explains that 42 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY Much more characteristic was the gradual extension of Roman direct rule.

The text explains that the great urban centres that lined the shores of the Mediterranean (Carthage, Alexandria, Antioch were the biggest among them) were the true bedrock of the imperial system.

The text explains that in Iran a new and more aggressive dynasty emerged in 225 (they called themselves the ‘Sasanians’) and within just 15 years were expanding rapidly in the direction of the Euphrates.

The text explains that in a famous rock inscription cut in three languages, Shapur I, the Iranian ruler, claimed he had annihilated a Roman army of 60,000 and even captured the eastern capital of Antioch.

The text explains that for example, in Pompeii, which was buried in a volcanic eruption in 79 CE, there is strong evidence of widespread casual literacy.

The text explains that 51 from their estates, not counting the produce they consumed directly!

The text explains that 24-79 Life of the Elder Pliny; dies in the volcanic eruption of Vesuvius, which also buries the Roman town of Pompeii 66-70 The great Jewish revolt and capture of Jerusalem by Roman forces c.

The text explains that the transcontinental span of the Mongol empire also meant that the sources available to scholars are written in a vast number of languages.

The text explains that perhaps the most crucial are the sources in Chinese, Mongolian, Persian and Arabic, but vital materials are also available in Italian, Latin, French and Russian.

The text explains that even though his own lifetime was spent consolidating his hold over the Mongol tribes, leading and directing campaigns into adjoining areas in north China, Transoxiana, Afghanistan, eastern Iran and the Russian steppes, his descendants travelled further afield to fulfil Genghis Khan’s vision and create the largest empire the world had ever seen.

The text explains that there were extremes of temperature in the entire region: harsh, long winters followed by brief, dry summers.

The text explains that ethnic and language ties united the Mongol people but the scarce resources meant that their society was divided into patrilineal lineages; the richer families were larger, possessed more animals and pasture lands.

The text explains that in fact, the scant resources of the steppe lands drove Mongols and other Central Asian nomads to trade and barter with their sedentary neighbours in China.

The text explains that commerce was not without its tensions, especially as the two groups unhesitatingly applied military pressure to enhance profit.

The text explains that in the 1230s, for example, as the Mongols waged their successful war against the Chin dynasty in north China, there was a strong pressure group within the Mongol leadership that advocated the massacre of all peasantry and the conversion of their fields into pasture lands.

The text explains that the pressure to sedentarise was greater in the new areas of Mongol domicile, areas distant from the original steppe habitat of the nomads.

The text explains that 4. ‘If history relies upon written records produced by city-based literati, nomadic societies will always receive a hostile representation.’ Would you agree with this statement?

The text explains that does it explain the reason why Persian chronicles produced such inflated figures of casualties resulting from Mongol campaigns?

The text explains that keeping the nomadic element of the Mongol and Bedouin societies in mind, how, in your opinion, did their respective historical experiences differ?

The text explains that what explanations would you suggest account for these differences?

The text explains that how does the following account enlarge upon the character of the Pax Mongolica created by the Mongols by the middle of the thirteenth century?

The text explains that the Franciscan monk, William of Rubruck, was sent by Louis IX of France on an embassy to the great Khan Mongke’s court.

The text explains that he reached Karakorum, the capital of Mongke, in 1254 and came upon a woman from Lorraine (in France) called Paquette, who had been brought from Hungary and was in the service of one of the prince’s wives who was a Nestorian Christian.

The text explains that at the court he came across a Parisian goldsmith named Guillaume Boucher, ‘whose brother dwelt on the Grand Pont in Paris’.

The text explains that this man was first employed by the Queen Sorghaqtani and then by Mongke’s younger brother.

The text explains that rubruck found that at the great court festivals the Nestorian priests were admitted first, with their regalia, to bless the Grand Khan’s cup, and were followed by the Muslim clergy and Buddhist and Taoist monks… Qubilai Khan and Chabi in camp.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Themes in World History, Chapter 2: An Empire Across Three Continents

schemes.point: The Roman Empire, by contrast, was a mosaic of territories and cultures that were chiefly bound together by a common system of government. (NCERT Class 11 Themes in World History Ch2 pg 11)

schemes.name: Next
schemes.point: Next to the emperor and the Senate, the other key institution of imperial rule was the army. (NCERT Class 11 Themes in World History Ch2 pg 11)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 30 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY and EMPIRES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to An Empire Across Three Continents; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain An Empire Across Three Continents through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 30 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY and EMPIRES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
